card.intuition: A three-dimensional conceptualization of HTML elements along an imaginary z-axis relative to the user. It dictates which elements appear 'on top' of others.
card.analogy: A folder system: if element B is inside Folder 1, and Folder 2 has a higher `z-index` than Folder 1, then EVERYTHING in Folder 2 will be on top of element B, even if element B has a huge `z-index` of 9999.
when_to_use: Fixing bugs where modals or tooltips appear behind other elements
Ensuring fixed headers stay on top of scrolling content
gotchas: Setting `opacity` less than 1, `transform`, or `filter` on an element creates a NEW stacking context automatically
The `z-index` property ONLY works on elements that have a `position` other than `static` (like relative, absolute, or fixed)

steps.label: Triggers
steps.annotation: Stacking context created by: position+z-index, opacity<1, transform, filter.

steps.label: Local order
steps.annotation: z-index only competes within the same stacking context — not globally.

steps.label: Paint order
steps.annotation: Background → negative z → block flow → floats → inline → z=0 → positive z.

steps.label: Isolation
steps.annotation: isolation: isolate forces a new context — prevents z-index fights with other layers.

steps.label: Debug
steps.annotation: Chrome DevTools Layers panel visualises each stacking context as a layer.

exercise.prompt: Identify which element will be on top. Element A is z-index 10. Element B is z-index 1 but is inside a parent with z-index 20. Neither creates a new context except the parent.
hints: Compare the stacking contexts, not the element's own z-index
B's parent has z-index 20, which beats A's z-index 10
